title: Conservation of Energy
summary: Energy changes form but never disappears — budget it down a rough ramp, heat and all.

steps.title: Energy is never lost
steps.body:
Energy doesn't appear or disappear — it just changes form. A roller coaster at the top of the hill is all **potential energy**; at the bottom it's all **kinetic**. The total never changed.

This is the big idea of the whole unit.

steps.title: The rule
steps.body:
When only **conservative** forces (like gravity or springs) act, mechanical energy stays constant — KE and PE just trade back and forth.

Slide the ball down the frictionless ramp below — watch potential energy pour into kinetic energy while the total bar never moves.

steps.prompt: A ball is released from rest at the top of a **frictionless** ramp, where it has **PE_g = 30 J** (and KE = 0). Drag the **total mechanical energy** it has at the bottom.
hints: With no friction, mechanical energy is conserved — the total can't grow or shrink.
The energy just changes form (PE → KE). The total at the bottom equals the total at the top.
steps.solution: On a frictionless ramp, mechanical energy is conserved: KE + PE stays constant. At the top it's all potential (30 J); at the bottom it's all kinetic — but the total never changed. So the total mechanical energy at the bottom is **30 J**.

steps.title: When friction joins in
steps.body:
Friction doesn't destroy energy — it turns mechanical energy into **thermal energy (E_th)** that heats the surfaces. The amount lost is just the friction force acting over the distance slid: E_th = F_f·d.

So the full energy balance for a slide down a ramp is:

steps.prompt:
**Setup:** A **2.0 kg** sled starts from rest and slides down a **4.0 m**-long ramp, dropping a vertical height of **2.0 m**. Friction turns **10 J** of energy into heat.

**Part A —** How much gravitational PE does the sled have at the top? (g = 9.8)
hints: Use the vertical height, not the 4.0 m along the ramp.
steps.solution: Gravitational PE uses the vertical height: PE_g = mgh = 2.0 × 9.8 × 2.0 = **39.2 J**.

steps.prompt: **Part B —** How much kinetic energy does the **2.0 kg** sled have at the bottom, after friction takes its cut?
hints: Energy balances as PE_g = KE + E_th. Solve for KE.
steps.solution:
The energy balance is PE_g = KE + E_th, so KE = PE_g − E_th.

KE = 39.2 − 10 = **29.2 J**. (Without friction it would have been the full 39.2 J.)

steps.prompt: **Part C —** How fast is the **2.0 kg** sled moving at the bottom?
hints: The kinetic energy at the bottom sets the speed: ½mv² = KE.
steps.solution:
Kinetic energy gives the speed: ½mv² = KE.

v = √(2·KE / m) = √(2·29.2 / 2.0) = √29.2 ≈ **5.4 m/s**.

steps.prompt: **Part D —** The sled slid **4.0 m** along the ramp while losing 10 J to heat. What was the average friction force?
hints: Heat generated equals the friction force times the distance slid: E_th = F_f·d.
Solve for F_f: F_f = E_th / d = 10 / 4.0.
steps.solution:
The thermal energy is the friction force acting over the slide distance: E_th = F_f·d.

F_f = E_th / d = 10 / 4.0 = **2.5 N**.

steps.title: Lesson complete!
steps.body: You ran a full energy budget down a rough ramp — PE in, heat out, motion left over — and even backed out the friction force.
